enrichment.short_description: Apple is a global consumer technology company that designs iPhone, Mac, iPad, and Apple Watch hardware alongside a services ecosystem spanning the App Store, iCloud, and Apple Business tools.
enrichment.seo_title: Apple 2026: Products, Services and Ecosystem Overview
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Apple designs iPhone, Mac, iPad and Apple Watch hardware plus App Store, iCloud and Apple Business services. See its history, scale and pricing model for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Apple Inc. is one of the world's largest technology companies, founded on April 1, 1976 by Steve Jobs, Steve Wozniak, and Ronald Wayne, and headquartered in Cupertino, California. Apple designs and sells consumer hardware including the iPhone, Mac computers, iPad tablets, Apple Watch, AirPods, and Apple Vision Pro, and it operates an extensive software and services ecosystem around that hardware: iOS, iPadOS, macOS, watchOS, the App Store, iCloud, Apple Music, Apple TV+, Apple Pay, and iMessage. For businesses and developers specifically, Apple offers Apple Business Essentials and Apple Business, which bundle device management (MDM), 24/7 support, and iCloud storage into subscription plans aimed at small and mid-sized organizations, alongside Apple Business Manager for enterprise device and app deployment, and the Apple Developer Program for building and distributing software on Apple platforms. Apple is a publicly traded company (NASDAQ: AAPL) with a market capitalization in the trillions of dollars, trailing twelve month revenue around 451 billion US dollars, and roughly 166,000 employees worldwide. Because Apple's catalog spans everything from a few hundred dollars for accessories to several thousand dollars for professional Mac hardware, there is no single 'starting price' for the company; instead, Apple's business model rests on two pillars: one-time hardware sales with premium margins, and a fast growing Services segment (App Store commissions, subscriptions like iCloud+, Apple Music, and Apple TV+, AppleCare, Apple Pay, and advertising) that now contributes roughly a quarter of total revenue at gross margins north of 75 percent, more than double what hardware earns. Services are typically sold as monthly or annual subscriptions, often bundled through Apple One, while hardware is sold outright or through installment/trade-in financing. In the context of a software and SaaS directory, Apple is most relevant as the maker of the operating systems and app distribution platforms (iOS, macOS, App Store) that nearly every other software product must integrate with or be distributed through, as well as a growing provider of business-facing device management and productivity services that compete with Google Workspace and Microsoft 365 in some respects, though Apple's core identity remains a hardware-first consumer electronics company with an increasingly important recurring-revenue services layer.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Apple
paragraphs: Apple is a multinational technology company founded in 1976 by Steve Jobs, Steve Wozniak, and Ronald Wayne. Headquartered in Cupertino, California, Apple designs, manufactures, and markets consumer electronics, computer software, and online services, and it is one of the most valuable companies in the world.

The company's product line spans the iPhone smartphone, Mac desktop and laptop computers, iPad tablets, Apple Watch, AirPods, Apple TV, and Apple Vision Pro, all built around Apple's own operating systems: iOS, iPadOS, macOS, watchOS, and visionOS.

Beyond hardware, Apple operates a large services business, including the App Store, iCloud, Apple Music, Apple TV+, Apple Arcade, Apple Pay, and business-oriented offerings such as Apple Business Essentials and Apple Business Manager for organizations managing fleets of Apple devices.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Apple's ecosystem is defined by tight integration between hardware, software, and services, letting devices like iPhone, Mac, and Apple Watch work together through features such as Handoff, Universal Clipboard, and iCloud sync.

For developers and businesses, Apple provides the App Store as a distribution channel reaching over a billion devices, the Apple Developer Program for building and shipping software, and device management tools for IT administrators.

Apple's services layer includes cloud storage (iCloud), payments (Apple Pay), media subscriptions (Apple Music, Apple TV+, Apple Arcade), and security and privacy features that are marketed as a core differentiator versus competitors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Apple does not have a single price point; hardware ranges from budget accessories to several-thousand-dollar professional Mac and Vision Pro configurations, and pricing varies by product, configuration, region, and carrier or financing arrangement.

Apple's services are generally sold as recurring subscriptions, often billed monthly, with bundles like Apple One combining multiple services (music, video, storage, arcade) at a discount versus buying each separately.

Business customers can subscribe to Apple Business Essentials, which combines device management, support, and iCloud storage into per-user, per-month plans, reflecting Apple's broader shift toward recurring services revenue alongside its traditional hardware sales model.

features.title: Integrated hardware lineup
features.description: iPhone, Mac, iPad, Apple Watch, AirPods, and Apple Vision Pro are designed to work together within a single ecosystem.

features.title: App Store distribution
features.description: A global marketplace for iOS, iPadOS, and macOS apps, reaching well over a billion active devices.

features.title: iCloud storage and sync
features.description: Cloud storage and synchronization for photos, documents, backups, and settings across Apple devices.

features.title: Apple Business Essentials
features.description: Subscription bundles for small businesses that combine mobile device management, 24/7 support, and iCloud storage.

features.title: Apple Pay
features.description: A contactless and in-app payments platform built into iPhone, Apple Watch, iPad, and Mac.

features.title: Developer tools and Xcode
features.description: A full development environment and SDKs for building apps across Apple's platforms.

features.title: Privacy and security features
features.description: Device-level encryption, App Tracking Transparency, and privacy-focused defaults across Apple's operating systems.

features.title: Media and entertainment subscriptions
features.description: Apple Music, Apple TV+, and Apple Arcade offer subscription access to music, video, and games.

pros: Deep integration across hardware, software, and services creates a cohesive user experience
Strong brand reputation for build quality, design, and long-term software support
Large, mature App Store ecosystem with a global developer and user base
Growing services portfolio generates high-margin recurring revenue
Privacy and security are treated as first-class product features
cons: Premium pricing on most hardware compared to many competitors
Closed ecosystem can make switching away from Apple products costly or inconvenient
App Store commission structure has drawn regulatory and developer criticism
No single, simple price list because offerings span dozens of product lines and configurations
Some services and features are only available within the Apple ecosystem, limiting cross-platform flexibility

faqs.question: What does Apple actually sell
faqs.answer: Apple sells consumer hardware such as iPhone, Mac, iPad, Apple Watch, and AirPods, plus a growing range of digital services including the App Store, iCloud, Apple Music, Apple TV+, and Apple Pay.

faqs.question: Who founded Apple and when
faqs.answer: Apple was founded on April 1, 1976 by Steve Jobs, Steve Wozniak, and Ronald Wayne.

faqs.question: Where is Apple headquartered
faqs.answer: Apple's headquarters, known as Apple Park, is located in Cupertino, California, United States.

faqs.question: How much does Apple cost
faqs.answer: There is no single Apple price since it sells many product lines at different price points, plus recurring subscription services; pricing varies by product, configuration, and region.

faqs.question: Does Apple offer business or enterprise plans
faqs.answer: Yes. Apple Business Essentials and Apple Business Manager provide subscription-based device management, support, and storage plans aimed at small and mid-sized organizations.

faqs.question: How big is Apple as a company
faqs.answer: Apple employs roughly 166,000 people worldwide and generates trailing twelve month revenue of approximately 451 billion US dollars, making it one of the largest companies in the world by market capitalization.

faqs.question: Is Apple a software company or a hardware company
faqs.answer: Apple is best described as an integrated hardware and services company; hardware sales remain the largest revenue source, but its Services segment, including the App Store and subscriptions, is a fast-growing, high-margin part of the business.

use_cases.title: Personal computing and communication
use_cases.description: Individuals use iPhone, Mac, and iPad for everyday communication, productivity, photography, and entertainment within a synced ecosystem.

use_cases.title: App development and distribution
use_cases.description: Developers build and publish apps through Xcode and the App Store to reach Apple's global user base.

use_cases.title: Business device management
use_cases.description: Organizations use Apple Business Essentials and Apple Business Manager to provision, secure, and support fleets of Apple devices for employees.
use_cases.audience: Small and mid-sized businesses, IT administrators

enrichment.short_description: Apply.build is a Platform-as-a-Service by Finland-based Codebite Oy that lets developers deploy applications from a GitHub repository directly to EU-hosted infrastructure without configuring CI/CD pipelines.
enrichment.seo_title: Apply.build 2026 Review: EU Deployment PaaS Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Apply.build is a Finland-based PaaS for deploying GitHub repos to EU-hosted infrastructure with automatic TLS, WAF and GDPR-compliant data residency.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Apply.build is a Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) operated by Codebite Oy, a Finnish company, designed for developers who want to deploy applications to infrastructure hosted and operated within Finland, with an emphasis on European data sovereignty and GDPR compliance. The core workflow is simple: a developer connects a GitHub repository, and pushes to the main branch trigger an automatic deployment, with the platform explicitly marketed as requiring no CI/CD pipeline configuration. Beyond basic deployment, Apply.build bundles in operational and security features that many small teams would otherwise have to assemble themselves, including a Web Application Firewall (WAF), automated vulnerability scanning for known CVEs, performance metrics and logging, and automatic TLS/SSL certificate provisioning. The platform reports serving more than 800 developers with a historical uptime figure around 99.94 percent. Pricing follows a freemium, resource-based model rather than per-seat licensing: a free tier provides 0.5 vCPU and 512 MiB of RAM with no credit card required, while paid usage is billed at EUR 5 per month per resource package of 1 vCPU and 1 GiB of RAM, with additional packages available as an application scales. This positions Apply.build as a lightweight alternative to larger, more complex PaaS and cloud platforms (such as Heroku, Render, Railway, or raw AWS/GCP/Azure deployments) for developers and small teams who specifically want their application and data to reside on EU infrastructure without manually configuring compliance, firewalls, or TLS themselves. Public information about the company's founding year, team size, and funding history is limited; Codebite Oy does not appear to have widely published this data, so those details should be treated as unverified rather than assumed. Apply.build is best understood as a niche, EU-first deployment platform aimed at GDPR-conscious developers and small businesses rather than a general-purpose global cloud provider, and it competes most directly with other simplified, GitHub-integrated PaaS tools rather than with hyperscale cloud vendors.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Apply.build
paragraphs: Apply.build is a Platform-as-a-Service operated by the Finnish company Codebite Oy. It is built specifically around deploying applications to infrastructure hosted and operated in Finland, targeting developers and teams that want EU data residency by default.
Rather than requiring users to configure a CI/CD pipeline, Apply.build connects directly to a GitHub repository and automatically deploys the application whenever code is pushed to the main branch.

The platform bundles operational essentials, including a Web Application Firewall, CVE vulnerability scanning, performance metrics, logging, and automatic TLS certificates, so that GDPR-conscious teams do not have to configure these separately.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: GitHub integration automatically redeploys an application whenever new code lands on the main branch, removing the need for a separate build pipeline.
Built-in security tooling includes a Web Application Firewall and automated scanning for known vulnerabilities (CVEs), aimed at teams without a dedicated security function.
Automatic TLS/SSL certificate issuance and renewal is handled by the platform, and EU-based hosting in Finland supports GDPR-aligned data residency requirements.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Apply.build uses a freemium, resource-based pricing model. The free tier includes 0.5 vCPU and 512 MiB of RAM and does not require a credit card to start.
Paid usage is billed per resource package: EUR 5 per month buys 1 vCPU and 1 GiB of RAM, and teams can add further resource packages as their application's traffic or workload grows.
This pay-for-resources approach differs from flat per-seat SaaS pricing, and it is aimed at developers who want predictable, small-scale hosting costs rather than enterprise cloud billing complexity.

features.title: One-push GitHub deployment
features.description: Connect a GitHub repository and deploy automatically on every push to the main branch, with no CI/CD configuration required.

features.title: EU-hosted infrastructure
features.description: Applications run on infrastructure hosted and operated in Finland, supporting GDPR-aligned data residency.

features.title: Web Application Firewall
features.description: Built-in WAF protection helps guard deployed applications from common web attacks.

features.title: Vulnerability scanning
features.description: Automated scanning checks deployed applications and dependencies for known CVEs.

features.title: Automatic TLS certificates
features.description: SSL/TLS certificates are provisioned and renewed automatically for deployed applications.

features.title: Performance metrics and logging
features.description: Built-in observability tools give developers visibility into application performance and logs.

features.title: Resource-based scaling
features.description: Additional vCPU and RAM can be added in resource packages as an application's needs grow.

pros: Simple GitHub-to-deploy workflow with no pipeline configuration needed
EU data residency in Finland is attractive for GDPR-focused teams
Free tier available with no credit card required
Security features like WAF and CVE scanning are included rather than sold separately
Transparent, resource-based pricing starting at a low monthly cost
cons: Limited public information about the company's founding, team size, and funding
Niche EU-only hosting footprint may not suit teams needing a global multi-region presence
Smaller ecosystem and community compared to established PaaS providers like Heroku or Render
Resource packages may require careful monitoring to avoid unexpected scaling costs
Best documented for GitHub-based workflows, with less clarity on support for other source control platforms

faqs.question: What is Apply.build
faqs.answer: Apply.build is a Platform-as-a-Service that automatically deploys applications from a GitHub repository to infrastructure hosted in Finland, aimed at developers who want simple, EU-based hosting.

faqs.question: Who operates Apply.build
faqs.answer: Apply.build is operated by Codebite Oy, a Finnish company.

faqs.question: How much does Apply.build cost
faqs.answer: Apply.build offers a free tier with 0.5 vCPU and 512 MiB RAM, and paid resource packages starting at EUR 5 per month for 1 vCPU and 1 GiB RAM.

faqs.question: Do I need to configure CI/CD to use Apply.build
faqs.answer: No. Apply.build automatically deploys an application when code is pushed to the main branch of a connected GitHub repository, without requiring a separate CI/CD pipeline.

faqs.question: Is Apply.build GDPR compliant
faqs.answer: Apply.build markets itself around GDPR-compliant EU data residency by hosting applications on infrastructure operated in Finland.

faqs.question: Does Apply.build include security features
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes a Web Application Firewall and automated vulnerability (CVE) scanning as part of the platform.

faqs.question: Is there a free plan for Apply.build
faqs.answer: Yes, a free tier with 0.5 vCPU and 512 MiB of RAM is available without requiring a credit card.

use_cases.title: GDPR-focused application hosting
use_cases.description: Teams that must keep application infrastructure within the EU use Apply.build to deploy without managing their own compliant hosting setup.

use_cases.title: Simplified deployment for small teams
use_cases.description: Developers without dedicated DevOps resources use Apply.build to deploy directly from GitHub without building a CI/CD pipeline.
use_cases.audience: Indie developers and small engineering teams

use_cases.title: Lightweight production hosting with built-in security
use_cases.description: Projects that need a WAF, vulnerability scanning, and TLS out of the box use Apply.build instead of assembling these tools separately.

features: 1 vCPU and 1 GiB RAM per package
WAF, CVE scanning, automatic TLS included

enrichment.short_description: AppNexus was a programmatic advertising exchange founded in 2007, acquired by AT&T in 2018 and rebranded as Xandr; its assets are now part of Microsoft Advertising after Microsoft acquired Xandr in 2022.
enrichment.headquarters: New York City, New York, United States
enrichment.seo_title: AppNexus in 2026: What Happened to It and Where It Is Now
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AppNexus was acquired by AT&T in 2018, rebranded Xandr, then sold to Microsoft in 2022. See its history and current status inside Microsoft Advertising.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AppNexus was an independent programmatic advertising exchange founded in September 2007 by Brian O'Kelley and Mike Nolet, headquartered in New York City. It became one of the largest ad exchanges and demand/supply-side platforms in the advertising technology industry, raising roughly 250 million US dollars from investors including Microsoft, Khosla Ventures, First Round Capital, Venrock, Marc Andreessen, and Ben Horowitz. In June 2018, AT&T acquired AppNexus for a reported 1.6 billion US dollars to build out its advertising business, and in September 2018 AT&T folded AppNexus into a newly formed unit called Xandr, which also absorbed AT&T's other ad tech assets. Xandr rebranded the AppNexus demand-side platform as Xandr Invest and continued operating the exchange and supply-side tools under the Xandr name. In December 2021, AT&T agreed to sell Xandr, including the former AppNexus technology, to Microsoft; the deal closed in June 2022. Microsoft initially operated the business as Xandr before retiring the Xandr brand in 2023 and folding its functions into Microsoft Advertising. As of 2026, the AppNexus lineage has effectively ended as a standalone brand: Microsoft announced it would shut down Microsoft Invest, the demand-side platform descended from the original AppNexus DSP, effective February 28, 2026, shifting its ad-buying strategy toward a Copilot-powered, conversational and agentic advertising product. The supply-side and publisher-facing ad tech that also traces back to AppNexus continues to operate under the Microsoft Monetize brand. For a software directory, AppNexus should therefore be presented as a historically important, now-defunct independent brand rather than an active current product; anyone searching for 'AppNexus' today is generally looking for either its acquisition history or its modern successor technology inside Microsoft's advertising stack, not a live, independently purchasable product with its own pricing or sign-up flow.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Was AppNexus
paragraphs: AppNexus was a programmatic advertising exchange and technology platform founded in 2007 by Brian O'Kelley and Mike Nolet in New York City. It grew into one of the largest independent ad exchanges, providing both demand-side (buying) and supply-side (selling) technology for digital advertising.
AT&T acquired AppNexus in June 2018 for a reported 1.6 billion US dollars and used it as the foundation for a new advertising unit called Xandr, launched later that year.

In December 2021, AT&T agreed to sell Xandr, including the former AppNexus assets, to Microsoft; the sale closed in June 2022, and Microsoft retired the Xandr brand in 2023, folding the technology into Microsoft Advertising.

long_form_blocks.heading: What the Technology Became
paragraphs: The original AppNexus demand-side platform was rebranded first as Xandr Invest and later as Microsoft Invest, providing programmatic ad-buying tools for agencies and advertisers.
Microsoft announced it would shut down Microsoft Invest effective February 28, 2026, moving toward a Copilot-powered, conversational approach to ad buying rather than a traditional DSP interface.
The supply-side and publisher ad serving technology descended from AppNexus continues to operate today under the Microsoft Monetize brand, separate from the now-discontinued demand-side product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing and Current Availability
paragraphs: AppNexus does not exist as an independently purchasable product; there is no current AppNexus pricing page, sign-up flow, or standalone brand to evaluate.

Historical AppNexus and Xandr pricing followed typical ad-tech norms, such as percentage-of-spend fees and platform/technology fees, but these are no longer publicly relevant given the brand's retirement.
Businesses looking for equivalent functionality today should evaluate Microsoft Advertising and Microsoft Monetize directly, rather than searching for AppNexus-specific pricing.

features.title: Programmatic ad exchange (historical)
features.description: AppNexus operated a real-time bidding exchange connecting advertisers and publishers, a foundational piece of early programmatic advertising.

features.title: Demand-side platform lineage
features.description: The AppNexus DSP evolved into Xandr Invest and later Microsoft Invest before being discontinued in February 2026.

features.title: Supply-side platform lineage
features.description: AppNexus's publisher-facing ad serving technology lives on today as part of Microsoft Monetize.

features.title: Acquisition by AT&T
features.description: AT&T bought AppNexus in 2018 to build a national TV and digital advertising marketplace under the Xandr brand.

features.title: Acquisition by Microsoft
features.description: Microsoft acquired Xandr, including AppNexus technology, from AT&T in a deal that closed in 2022.

features.title: Brand retirement
features.description: Microsoft phased out the Xandr name in 2023 and is retiring the Invest DSP entirely in 2026, ending the AppNexus product lineage.

pros: Historically influential in pioneering real-time bidding and programmatic advertising
Backed by major strategic and venture investors before its acquisition
Underlying supply-side technology continues to operate today under Microsoft Monetize
Acquisition history is well documented, giving buyers clarity on where the technology went
cons: No longer exists as an independent, purchasable product or brand
The demand-side platform descended from AppNexus is being shut down in 2026
Anyone searching for AppNexus today must instead evaluate Microsoft Advertising products
Historical pricing and packaging are not representative of any current offering

faqs.question: Does AppNexus still exist
faqs.answer: Not as an independent brand. AppNexus was acquired by AT&T in 2018, rebranded Xandr, then sold to Microsoft in 2022 and folded into Microsoft Advertising, with the Xandr name retired in 2023.

faqs.question: What happened to the AppNexus demand-side platform
faqs.answer: It became Xandr Invest and later Microsoft Invest; Microsoft announced it would discontinue Microsoft Invest effective February 28, 2026, in favor of a Copilot-powered advertising approach.

faqs.question: Is any part of AppNexus still active today
faqs.answer: The supply-side, publisher-facing ad technology that descended from AppNexus continues to operate under the Microsoft Monetize brand.

faqs.question: Who founded AppNexus
faqs.answer: AppNexus was founded in 2007 by Brian O'Kelley and Mike Nolet in New York City.

faqs.question: Why did AT&T buy AppNexus
faqs.answer: AT&T acquired AppNexus in 2018 for a reported 1.6 billion US dollars to build a national advertising marketplace, which it launched as Xandr.

faqs.question: Can I still sign up for AppNexus
faqs.answer: No. There is no current AppNexus product, pricing page, or sign-up flow; the technology now lives inside Microsoft Advertising and Microsoft Monetize.

faqs.question: What replaced Xandr as a brand
faqs.answer: Microsoft retired the Xandr name in 2023 and consolidated its advertising technology, including the former AppNexus assets, under the Microsoft Advertising brand.

use_cases.title: Historical reference for ad tech research
use_cases.description: Analysts and historians reference AppNexus as a foundational company in the development of programmatic advertising and real-time bidding.
use_cases.audience: Ad tech researchers and industry analysts

use_cases.title: Understanding current Microsoft Advertising products
use_cases.description: Buyers evaluating Microsoft's advertising stack benefit from understanding that its supply-side technology traces back to AppNexus.
use_cases.audience: Advertisers and publishers evaluating Microsoft Advertising

use_cases.title: Due diligence on acquired ad tech brands
use_cases.description: Investors and business development teams researching ad tech consolidation use AppNexus as a case study in exchange acquisitions.

enrichment.short_description: Appointy is a cloud-based online appointment scheduling and booking platform for salons, healthcare providers, fitness studios, and other service businesses, offering a free tier and paid plans starting under 20 dollars a month.
enrichment.seo_title: Appointy Review 2026: Scheduling Software Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Appointy is online appointment scheduling software for salons, healthcare, and fitness businesses. Compare 2026 pricing plans, features, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Appointy is a cloud-based online appointment scheduling and booking platform founded in 2006 by Nemesh Singh, with its origins and a significant part of its operations based in India. The company positions itself for service-based businesses that need to manage appointments, staff calendars, and payments online, with common customer segments including salons and spas, healthcare and wellness providers, fitness studios, tutoring and education businesses, and other appointment-driven small businesses. Appointy reports serving more than 110,000 customers globally. The product's core functionality includes an embeddable online booking widget, staff and resource scheduling, calendar synchronization with tools like Google Calendar, automated reminders, payment processing, and reporting. Appointy uses a freemium, per-business (rather than strictly per-user) pricing model: a Free plan offers a limited feature set (a small number of services and a monthly appointment cap with no staff seats), a Growth plan starts at 19.99 US dollars per month (billed annually; 29.99 US dollars month-to-month) for a single staff member with unlimited appointments and services plus email and live chat support, a Professional plan around 49.99 US dollars per month adds access for five staff members and resource scheduling, and an Enterprise plan around 79.99 US dollars per month supports multiple locations, premium support, branding removal, and custom text messaging. All plans are offered without long-term contracts, and a 14-day free trial is available without requiring a credit card. Appointy's most direct competitors in the appointment scheduling space are Calendly, Acuity Scheduling (by Squarespace), and Square Appointments; compared to Calendly, which is strongest for simple meeting scheduling, Appointy leans more toward multi-staff, multi-location service businesses like salons and clinics that need resource and staff management alongside booking, similar in positioning to Acuity Scheduling. The company has also made small acquisitions, including a January 2023 deal for Zyoga, a yoga-studio-focused scheduling tool, reflecting a strategy of expanding into vertical-specific scheduling niches. Reported employee counts vary widely across data providers, from roughly two dozen to a couple hundred, so exact company size should be treated as uncertain rather than precisely known.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Appointy
paragraphs: Appointy is a cloud-based appointment scheduling and online booking platform founded in 2006 by Nemesh Singh. It is designed for service businesses such as salons, spas, healthcare and wellness clinics, fitness studios, and tutoring services that need to manage bookings, staff calendars, and payments in one place.
The platform lets businesses embed a booking widget on their website, accept appointments 24/7, manage multiple staff members and locations, and sync with external calendars such as Google Calendar.
Appointy reports more than 110,000 customers worldwide and has made small acquisitions, including yoga-studio scheduling tool Zyoga in 2023, to expand into specific vertical markets.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core scheduling features include an online booking widget, automated appointment reminders via email and text, staff and resource management, and calendar synchronization.
Appointy supports payment processing so businesses can collect deposits or full payment at the time of booking, along with reporting on bookings, revenue, and staff utilization.
Higher tiers add multi-location support, dedicated staff logins, custom branding removal, and priority support, making the platform scalable from a solo practitioner up to a multi-location business.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Appointy offers a Free plan with a capped number of services and monthly appointments and no dedicated staff seats, aimed at very small or single-operator businesses testing the platform.

The Growth plan starts at 19.99 US dollars per month when billed annually (29.99 US dollars billed monthly) for one staff member with unlimited appointments and services, followed by a Professional plan around 49.99 US dollars per month for up to five staff members with resource scheduling.

The Enterprise plan, around 79.99 US dollars per month, adds multi-location support, premium support, and removal of Appointy branding; all paid plans are offered without long-term contracts and a 14-day free trial is available without a credit card.

features.title: Online booking widget
features.description: An embeddable widget lets customers book appointments directly from a business's website 24 hours a day.

features.title: Staff and resource scheduling
features.description: Manage multiple staff members' calendars, availability, and bookable resources such as rooms or equipment.

features.title: Calendar sync
features.description: Two-way synchronization with calendars like Google Calendar keeps availability accurate across platforms.

features.title: Automated reminders
features.description: Email and text reminders reduce no-shows by notifying customers ahead of their booked appointment.

features.title: Payment processing
features.description: Accept deposits or full payments at the time of booking through integrated payment processing.

features.title: Multi-location support
features.description: Enterprise-tier businesses can manage scheduling across multiple locations from a single account.

features.title: Custom branding
features.description: Higher-tier plans allow businesses to remove Appointy branding from their booking pages.

features.title: Reporting and analytics
features.description: Built-in reports give visibility into bookings, revenue, and staff performance over time.

pros: Free plan available for very small businesses testing online booking
Per-business rather than strict per-seat pricing on some tiers keeps costs predictable
Wide range of supported industries, from salons to healthcare to fitness
No long-term contracts required on paid plans
14-day free trial with no credit card required
cons: Free plan is limited to a small number of services and monthly appointments
Multi-staff and multi-location features require moving up to higher-priced tiers
Reported company size varies significantly across data sources, making scale hard to verify
Less brand recognition in some markets compared to Calendly or Acuity Scheduling

faqs.question: What is Appointy used for
faqs.answer: Appointy is used by service businesses like salons, healthcare providers, and fitness studios to manage online appointment booking, staff calendars, and payments.

faqs.question: How much does Appointy cost
faqs.answer: Appointy has a free plan and paid plans starting at 19.99 US dollars per month (billed annually) on the Growth tier, scaling up to Professional and Enterprise plans for more staff and locations.

faqs.question: Does Appointy have a free trial
faqs.answer: Yes, Appointy offers a 14-day free trial that does not require a credit card, in addition to a permanently free limited plan.

faqs.question: How does Appointy compare to Calendly
faqs.answer: Calendly focuses on simple one-on-one meeting scheduling, while Appointy is built more for multi-staff, multi-location service businesses like salons and clinics that need resource and staff management alongside booking.

faqs.question: Who founded Appointy and when
faqs.answer: Appointy was founded in 2006 by Nemesh Singh.

faqs.question: Can Appointy process payments
faqs.answer: Yes, Appointy supports payment processing so businesses can collect deposits or full payment when a customer books an appointment.

faqs.question: Does Appointy support multiple business locations
faqs.answer: Yes, multi-location support is available on Appointy's Enterprise plan.

use_cases.title: Salon and spa booking
use_cases.description: Salons and spas use Appointy to let clients book services online, manage stylist schedules, and reduce no-shows with reminders.

use_cases.title: Healthcare and wellness scheduling
use_cases.description: Clinics and wellness practitioners use Appointy to manage patient appointments, staff availability, and intake reminders.

use_cases.title: Fitness studio class and session booking
use_cases.description: Fitness studios use Appointy to schedule classes, personal training sessions, and manage instructor calendars across locations.

enrichment.short_description: AppVeyor is a hosted continuous integration and deployment (CI/CD) service, historically known for strong Windows build support, offering paid plans from 29 dollars a month alongside a free tier for open-source projects.
enrichment.seo_title: AppVeyor 2026 Review: CI/CD Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AppVeyor is a Windows-focused CI/CD platform founded in 2011. See 2026 pricing plans, key features, and how it compares to GitHub Actions and CircleCI.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AppVeyor is a hosted continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) service founded in 2011 by Feodor Fitsner, a former Microsoft Azure engineer, and headquartered in Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada. It automates building, testing, and deploying software projects hosted on services like GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket, running jobs on Windows, Linux (Ubuntu), and macOS virtual machines. AppVeyor built its early reputation as one of the few CI services offering first-class, native Windows build support at a time when most competitors focused primarily on Linux, which made it a popular choice for .NET, Windows desktop, and cross-platform open-source projects that needed to verify Windows compatibility. The product remains actively available in 2026, though the broader CI/CD market has consolidated heavily around GitHub Actions (bundled directly into GitHub, the platform most projects already use), along with CircleCI, GitLab CI/CD, Azure DevOps, and Jenkins for self-hosted needs; AppVeyor is generally positioned today as a smaller, focused alternative rather than a market leader, appealing most to teams with existing AppVeyor pipelines or specific Windows-build requirements not as smoothly served elsewhere. Pricing is subscription-based with a free Open Source tier offering unlimited public projects with one concurrent job and five self-hosted job connections; paid plans start with Basic at 29 US dollars per month for one private project, scale to Pro at 59 US dollars per month (590 US dollars annually) for unlimited private projects, and Premium at 99 US dollars per month (990 US dollars annually) which adds a second concurrent job. Additional concurrent jobs cost 50 US dollars per month each, and additional self-hosted job packs cost 10 US dollars per month per five jobs; all plans cap individual build execution at 60 minutes, and a 50 percent discount is available for students, educational organizations, and open-source projects. A 14-day free trial is available with no credit card required. AppVeyor's target audience is primarily .NET, Windows, and cross-platform developers and small-to-mid-sized engineering teams who need reliable Windows CI, though many teams already on GitHub now default to GitHub Actions for simplicity, making AppVeyor a niche rather than a mainstream first choice for greenfield projects in 2026.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AppVeyor
paragraphs: AppVeyor is a hosted continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) platform founded in 2011 by Feodor Fitsner, headquartered in Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada. It automates building, testing, and deploying code hosted on GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, and other source control providers.

The platform runs build and test jobs on Windows, Linux, and macOS virtual machines, and it became particularly well known for offering strong, native Windows build support at a time when most CI services focused primarily on Linux.
AppVeyor is commonly used by .NET developers, Windows desktop application teams, and open-source projects that need to verify Windows compatibility alongside Linux and macOS builds.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: AppVeyor supports build matrices across Windows, Linux, and macOS, letting teams test the same codebase across multiple operating systems and runtime versions in parallel.
The platform integrates with popular source control providers (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket) and supports deployment to common targets, along with build artifact storage and test result reporting.
Self-hosted (on-premises) build agents are supported for teams that need to run jobs on their own infrastructure while still using AppVeyor's orchestration and reporting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: AppVeyor's Open Source plan is free and includes unlimited public projects, one concurrent job, and up to five self-hosted job connections, making it accessible for public open-source projects.

Paid plans start at Basic ($29/month) for one private project, then Pro ($59/month, or $590/year), and Premium ($99/month, or $990/year), which is the first tier to include two concurrent jobs instead of one.

Extra concurrent jobs cost $50/month each and extra self-hosted job packs cost $10/month per five jobs; all plans cap build execution at 60 minutes per job, with a 50 percent discount for students, educational organizations, and open-source projects, and a 14-day free trial with no credit card required.

features.title: Native Windows build support
features.description: First-class support for building and testing on Windows, historically a differentiator versus Linux-first CI services.

features.title: Cross-platform build matrix
features.description: Run builds and tests across Windows, Linux, and macOS virtual machines in parallel.

features.title: Source control integrations
features.description: Connects to GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, and other providers to trigger builds automatically on code changes.

features.title: Self-hosted build agents
features.description: Teams can run jobs on their own infrastructure while still managing pipelines through AppVeyor.

features.title: Artifact and test reporting
features.description: Store build artifacts and view test results directly within the AppVeyor dashboard.

features.title: Deployment integrations
features.description: Deploy build outputs to common targets as part of the CI/CD pipeline.

features.title: Free tier for open source
features.description: Public open-source projects can use AppVeyor for free with unlimited public projects.

pros: Strong, long-established Windows build support
Cross-platform builds across Windows, Linux, and macOS
Free tier available for open-source and public projects
Simple, transparent per-project pricing tiers
Discounts available for students and educational use
cons: Lower concurrency on lower tiers can slow down teams with many parallel builds
Faces heavy competition from GitHub Actions, which is bundled directly into GitHub
60-minute maximum build execution time per job may not suit very large builds
Smaller market share and community compared to GitHub Actions, CircleCI, or GitLab CI/CD
Extra concurrency and self-hosted job packs add incremental cost as teams scale

faqs.question: What is AppVeyor used for
faqs.answer: AppVeyor is a CI/CD platform used to automatically build, test, and deploy software projects, historically known for strong native Windows build support.

faqs.question: Is AppVeyor free to use
faqs.answer: AppVeyor offers a free Open Source plan with unlimited public projects and one concurrent job; paid plans for private projects start at $29/month.

faqs.question: How does AppVeyor compare to GitHub Actions
faqs.answer: GitHub Actions is built directly into GitHub and is the default choice for many teams already using GitHub, while AppVeyor is a separate, more Windows-focused CI service that some teams prefer for specific Windows build needs.

faqs.question: Who founded AppVeyor
faqs.answer: AppVeyor was founded in 2011 by Feodor Fitsner, a former Microsoft Azure engineer, and is headquartered in Vancouver, Canada.

faqs.question: Does AppVeyor support Linux and macOS builds
faqs.answer: Yes, in addition to Windows, AppVeyor supports building and testing on Linux (Ubuntu) and macOS virtual machines.

faqs.question: What is the maximum build time on AppVeyor
faqs.answer: All AppVeyor plans have a maximum build job execution time of 60 minutes.

faqs.question: Does AppVeyor offer a free trial for paid plans
faqs.answer: Yes, AppVeyor offers a 14-day free trial with no credit card required.

use_cases.title: Windows and .NET application testing
use_cases.description: .NET and Windows desktop application teams use AppVeyor to run native Windows builds and tests as part of their release process.

use_cases.title: Cross-platform open-source CI
use_cases.description: Open-source projects use AppVeyor's free tier to verify Windows compatibility alongside other CI services covering Linux and macOS.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted enterprise build pipelines
use_cases.description: Teams with compliance or infrastructure requirements use AppVeyor's self-hosted build agents to run jobs on their own servers.

enrichment.short_description: Aptana Studio was a free, open-source Eclipse-based IDE for web development, later owned by Appcelerator and then Axway; it has not received a stable release since 2018 and is effectively unmaintained today.
enrichment.seo_title: Aptana Studio in 2026: Is It Still Maintained or Discontinued
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Aptana Studio is a free, open-source web IDE now under Axway. See its history, features, and current unmaintained status heading into 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Aptana was a software company founded in 2005 by Paul Colton in San Mateo, California, best known for Aptana Studio, a free and open-source integrated development environment (IDE) built on the Eclipse platform for web application development, supporting HTML, CSS, JavaScript, PHP, Ruby, and Python among other languages. Aptana raised approximately 20.3 million US dollars in funding from investors including Accel and Rembrandt Partners, and Aptana Studio was downloaded more than 4 million times during its active years. In January 2011, Aptana was acquired by Appcelerator, a mobile app development platform company, which continued to develop and distribute Aptana Studio alongside its own Titanium mobile SDK products. In January 2016, Appcelerator itself was acquired by Axway, an enterprise API management and secure data exchange company, which has continued to list Aptana on its website (axway.com/en/aptana) primarily as a legacy download and reference page rather than an actively developed product line. As of the current date, Aptana Studio's last stable release remains version 3.7.2, published in July 2018, with no official updates, security patches, or new feature releases since that date. The public GitHub repository for Aptana Studio 3 remains available under the GNU General Public License version 3, allowing downloads, forks, and community contributions, but shows minimal official activity from Axway, with the most recent commits dating to around 2020 and no published roadmap for future development. In practice, Aptana Studio should be considered inactive/unmaintained rather than formally discontinued: the software still exists, can still be downloaded and run, and remains open source, but it receives no meaningful vendor support, security updates, or compatibility fixes for modern web development workflows. Developers evaluating Aptana Studio today are better served by actively maintained, modern alternatives such as Visual Studio Code, WebStorm, or Sublime Text, all of which offer current language support, extension ecosystems, and active security patching that Aptana Studio can no longer provide. There is no commercial pricing associated with Aptana Studio; it was and remains a free, open-source tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Aptana
paragraphs: Aptana was a software company founded in 2005 by Paul Colton, based in San Mateo, California, best known for Aptana Studio, a free, open-source, Eclipse-based IDE for web development covering HTML, CSS, JavaScript, PHP, Ruby, and Python.

Aptana was acquired by Appcelerator in January 2011, and Appcelerator was in turn acquired by Axway in January 2016; Axway has continued to host an Aptana product page, though development activity has slowed dramatically since the acquisition.

Aptana Studio's core value proposition was a full-featured, no-cost IDE for web developers at a time when many competing tools were commercial products, and it was downloaded more than 4 million times over its active life.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features (Historical)
paragraphs: Aptana Studio provided code assistance including autocomplete, syntax highlighting, and code outlining for HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, along with support for server-side languages like PHP, Ruby (including Ruby on Rails), and Python.
Because it was built on the Eclipse platform, Aptana Studio could use Eclipse plugins and offered integrated FTP/SFTP deployment, a built-in terminal, and Git and SVN version control integration.
The IDE also included debugging tools and a built-in preview capability for testing web pages during development, positioning it as an all-in-one environment rather than a lightweight editor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing and Current Status
paragraphs: Aptana Studio has always been free and open source, released under the GNU General Public License version 3, with no paid tiers or commercial licensing.
There is no current pricing to evaluate because Aptana Studio has not received a stable release since version 3.7.2 in July 2018, and Axway does not appear to be actively developing it further.

The source code remains publicly available on GitHub for anyone who wants to download, fork, or maintain a community version, but prospective users should treat the project as effectively unmaintained rather than actively supported software.

features.title: Eclipse-based IDE
features.description: Built on the Eclipse platform, giving Aptana Studio access to Eclipse's plugin ecosystem and extensibility.

features.title: Multi-language web support
features.description: Supported HTML, CSS, JavaScript, PHP, Ruby (including Rails), and Python development in a single environment.

features.title: Code assistance
features.description: Provided autocomplete, syntax highlighting, and code outlining to speed up front-end and back-end web development.

features.title: Built-in deployment tools
features.description: Included integrated FTP/SFTP support for deploying projects directly from the IDE.

features.title: Version control integration
features.description: Supported Git and SVN integration for source control directly within the IDE.

features.title: Free and open source
features.description: Released under GPLv3 with no cost, making it accessible to individual developers and small teams.

pros: Completely free and open source with no licensing cost
Broad multi-language support in a single IDE
Built on Eclipse, allowing use of the wider Eclipse plugin ecosystem
Source code remains available on GitHub for community use or forking
cons: No stable release since version 3.7.2 in July 2018
No official security updates or bug fixes for several years
Not actively maintained by Axway despite still being listed on its website
Lacks modern IDE features and extensions found in current tools like VS Code
Not recommended for production development workflows given its unmaintained status

faqs.question: Is Aptana Studio still maintained
faqs.answer: No, not actively. Aptana Studio's last stable release was version 3.7.2 in July 2018, and Axway, its current owner, does not appear to be developing it further.

faqs.question: Is Aptana Studio free
faqs.answer: Yes, Aptana Studio has always been free and open source under the GNU General Public License version 3.

faqs.question: Who owns Aptana now
faqs.answer: Aptana is owned by Axway, which acquired it indirectly through its 2016 acquisition of Appcelerator (which had acquired Aptana in 2011).

faqs.question: Can I still download Aptana Studio
faqs.answer: Yes, the source code and installers remain publicly available on GitHub, though the project receives minimal official updates.

faqs.question: Who created Aptana Studio
faqs.answer: Aptana was founded in 2005 by Paul Colton, and Aptana Studio was its flagship open-source web development IDE.

faqs.question: Should I use Aptana Studio for new projects in 2026
faqs.answer: Generally not recommended; given the lack of updates since 2018, actively maintained alternatives like Visual Studio Code or WebStorm are better suited for current web development.

faqs.question: What languages did Aptana Studio support
faqs.answer: Aptana Studio supported HTML, CSS, JavaScript, PHP, Ruby (including Ruby on Rails), and Python, among other web technologies.

use_cases.title: Legacy project maintenance
use_cases.description: Developers maintaining older codebases originally built in Aptana Studio may still open and edit them in the IDE.

use_cases.title: Educational or historical reference
use_cases.description: Students and researchers studying the history of web IDEs may explore Aptana Studio as an example of a once-popular free tool.

use_cases.title: Community-maintained forks
use_cases.description: Open-source contributors can fork the Aptana Studio GitHub repository to experiment with or revive parts of the project.

features: No official support or updates since 2018

enrichment.short_description: Apteligent, formerly Crittercism, was a mobile app performance monitoring and crash reporting platform acquired by VMware in 2017; its website now redirects into VMware Workspace ONE Intelligence, and it is no longer sold as a standalone product.
enrichment.seo_title: Apteligent in 2026: What Happened After the VMware Acquisition
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Apteligent, formerly Crittercism, was acquired by VMware in 2017. See its current status and how its mobile monitoring tech lives on in Workspace ONE.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Apteligent was a mobile application performance monitoring and crash reporting platform originally launched under the name Crittercism, headquartered in San Francisco, California. It provided real-time visibility into mobile app crashes, network performance, and user experience issues for iOS and Android applications, targeting mobile engineering and DevOps teams that needed to diagnose and fix app stability problems in production. The company rebranded from Crittercism to Apteligent before being acquired by VMware on May 15, 2017; VMware positioned the acquisition as extending its Digital Workspace strategy by adding native mobile app performance monitoring to complement its End-User Computing (EUC) product line, particularly Workspace ONE. Following the acquisition, Apteligent's technology was progressively integrated into VMware's Workspace ONE Intelligence platform rather than continuing to be sold or marketed as a standalone Apteligent product; the apteligent.com website now redirects directly to a VMware Workspace ONE Intelligence page covering consumer app analytics, confirming that Apteligent no longer operates as an independent brand or product with its own pricing, sign-up flow, or support channel. This mirrors the pattern seen with many mobile-era acquisitions where a point solution is absorbed into a larger platform's feature set rather than preserved as a separate SKU. The broader corporate chain has continued to shift since the original 2017 deal: VMware itself was acquired by Broadcom in a transaction that closed in 2023, and Broadcom has since restructured VMware's business toward subscription-only licensing across its unified Workspace ONE and related platforms, ending the sale of perpetual licenses in 2024. There is no publicly confirmed dedicated Apteligent pricing, roadmap, or support offering as of the current date; any organization interested in equivalent mobile app performance monitoring functionality would need to evaluate VMware Workspace ONE Intelligence directly, or consider actively maintained standalone alternatives such as Firebase Crashlytics, Sentry, Instabug, or Datadog Real User Monitoring, since Apteligent itself should be treated as a discontinued, folded-in brand rather than a currently purchasable product.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Was Apteligent
paragraphs: Apteligent was a mobile application performance monitoring and crash reporting platform, originally founded and operated under the name Crittercism, headquartered in San Francisco, California, before rebranding to Apteligent.

The platform gave mobile engineering teams real-time visibility into app crashes, network errors, and performance issues across iOS and Android, helping them diagnose and resolve stability problems affecting end users.
VMware acquired Apteligent on May 15, 2017, describing the deal as a way to extend its Digital Workspace and End-User Computing strategy with native mobile app performance monitoring capabilities.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Happened to the Technology
paragraphs: After the acquisition, Apteligent's monitoring capabilities were folded into VMware's Workspace ONE Intelligence platform rather than continuing to be sold as an independent, separately branded product.

The apteligent.com website today redirects visitors directly to a VMware Workspace ONE Intelligence page covering consumer app analytics, confirming there is no standalone Apteligent product, pricing page, or account sign-up anymore.
VMware itself was subsequently acquired by Broadcom, in a deal that closed in 2023, and Broadcom has since moved VMware's product lines, including Workspace ONE, toward subscription-only licensing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing and Current Availability
paragraphs: There is no current, independently confirmed pricing for Apteligent because it is no longer sold as a standalone product; its capabilities are addressed today through VMware Workspace ONE Intelligence.

Organizations that previously used Apteligent for mobile crash reporting and performance monitoring would need to evaluate Workspace ONE Intelligence's current packaging under Broadcom, or migrate to an actively maintained standalone alternative.
Given the discontinued, folded-in status of the Apteligent brand, it is not possible to list current pricing plans for it in a directory context without risking inaccurate information.

features.title: Mobile crash reporting (historical)
features.description: Apteligent captured and reported real-time crash data for iOS and Android applications.

features.title: Network performance monitoring (historical)
features.description: The platform tracked network call performance and errors affecting mobile app experience.

features.title: Real-time analytics dashboard (historical)
features.description: Provided engineering teams a dashboard to monitor app health and stability trends over time.

features.title: Rebrand from Crittercism
features.description: The company rebranded from Crittercism to Apteligent prior to its acquisition by VMware.

features.title: Acquisition by VMware
features.description: VMware acquired Apteligent in 2017 to extend its Digital Workspace and End-User Computing product strategy.

features.title: Folded into Workspace ONE Intelligence
features.description: Apteligent's monitoring capabilities now live inside VMware Workspace ONE Intelligence rather than as a standalone product.

pros: Historically provided real-time, detailed mobile crash and performance diagnostics
Acquisition by VMware validated the underlying technology's value for enterprise mobility
Core monitoring capabilities persist today within VMware Workspace ONE Intelligence
cons: No longer available as an independent, purchasable product
No public standalone pricing, support, or sign-up since being folded into Workspace ONE
Requires adopting the broader VMware Workspace ONE platform (now under Broadcom) to access similar capabilities
Not a viable option for teams wanting a lightweight, standalone crash reporting tool today

faqs.question: Does Apteligent still exist as a product
faqs.answer: No. Apteligent's website now redirects to VMware Workspace ONE Intelligence, and it is not sold as a standalone product.

faqs.question: What was Apteligent originally called
faqs.answer: Apteligent was originally known as Crittercism before rebranding.

faqs.question: Who acquired Apteligent
faqs.answer: VMware acquired Apteligent on May 15, 2017, to extend its Digital Workspace and End-User Computing strategy.

faqs.question: Is Apteligent part of Broadcom now
faqs.answer: Indirectly, yes. VMware, which owns the former Apteligent technology, was acquired by Broadcom in a deal that closed in 2023.

faqs.question: Can I still sign up for Apteligent
faqs.answer: No, there is no current Apteligent sign-up or pricing page; interested organizations should look at VMware Workspace ONE Intelligence instead.

faqs.question: What did Apteligent do
faqs.answer: Apteligent provided mobile app performance monitoring and crash reporting, giving engineering teams visibility into app stability and network performance issues.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to Apteligent today
faqs.answer: Actively maintained alternatives for mobile crash and performance monitoring include Firebase Crashlytics, Sentry, Instabug, and Datadog Real User Monitoring.

use_cases.title: Historical reference for mobile monitoring history
use_cases.description: Analysts researching the mobile app performance monitoring category may reference Apteligent and its acquisition by VMware.

use_cases.title: Understanding Workspace ONE Intelligence capabilities
use_cases.description: IT teams evaluating VMware Workspace ONE Intelligence may want to know that its consumer app analytics trace back to Apteligent.

use_cases.title: Migration planning for former Apteligent users
use_cases.description: Teams that previously relied on Apteligent for crash reporting need to evaluate Workspace ONE Intelligence or a standalone alternative.
use_cases.audience: Mobile engineering teams migrating monitoring tools

enrichment.short_description: Apttus was a contract lifecycle management (CLM) and configure-price-quote (CPQ) software company founded in 2006; it merged with Conga in 2020 and adopted the Conga name, so apttus.com now redirects to conga.com.
enrichment.seo_title: Apttus in 2026: Why It Is Now Called Conga
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Apttus was a CLM and CPQ software company that merged with Conga in 2020 and took the Conga name. See its history and where the brand stands today.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Apttus was a contract lifecycle management (CLM) and configure-price-quote (CPQ) software company founded in 2006 by Kirk Krappe, Neehar Giri, and Kent Perkocha, headquartered in San Mateo, California. The company was bootstrapped in its early years, taking no outside investment until 2013, when it raised a 37 million US dollar Series A round from investors including K1 Capital, ICONIQ, and Salesforce, reflecting its deep integration with the Salesforce ecosystem, on which many of its products were built as native applications. Apttus grew into one of the leading providers of quote-to-cash software, covering CPQ, CLM, revenue management, and related deal-desk and commercial operations tools, competing with vendors like Salesforce CPQ (formerly SteelBrick), Icertis, and DocuSign CLM. On May 7, 2020, Apttus announced it would acquire fellow quote-to-cash and document automation company Conga in a deal valued at approximately 715 million US dollars; notably, the combined company chose to adopt the Conga brand name going forward rather than keeping the Apttus name, with former Apttus CEO Frank Holland leading the newly combined Conga as CEO. The merged company reported roughly 400 million US dollars in combined GAAP revenue and more than 10,000 enterprise and mid-market customers at the time of the deal, spanning CPQ, CLM, document generation, process automation, and eSignature. As a result, Apttus no longer exists as an active, independently branded product or company; the apttus.com domain redirects (HTTP 301) directly to conga.com, and all of Apttus's former CPQ and CLM product lines are now marketed, sold, and supported under the Conga brand. For a software directory, Apttus should be presented clearly as a legacy brand name that customers and researchers may still search for, with the accurate current-state guidance being to evaluate Conga directly for CPQ, CLM, and quote-to-cash software, rather than presenting Apttus as a currently active, separately purchasable product with its own live pricing or sign-up process.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Was Apttus
paragraphs: Apttus was a contract lifecycle management (CLM) and configure-price-quote (CPQ) software company founded in 2006 by Kirk Krappe, Neehar Giri, and Kent Perkocha, headquartered in San Mateo, California.
The company built its products natively on the Salesforce platform and grew into a major quote-to-cash vendor, helping enterprises manage contracts, pricing, quoting, and revenue processes.
Apttus operated largely bootstrapped until 2013, when it raised a 37 million US dollar Series A round from investors including K1 Capital, ICONIQ, and Salesforce.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Merger with Conga
paragraphs: On May 7, 2020, Apttus announced it was acquiring Conga, another quote-to-cash and document automation vendor, in a deal valued at roughly 715 million US dollars.
Rather than keeping the Apttus name, the combined company chose to operate under the Conga brand, with former Apttus CEO Frank Holland leading the newly unified company.
The merger combined CPQ, CLM, document generation, process automation, and eSignature capabilities under one roof, serving more than 10,000 enterprise and mid-market customers at the time.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing and Current Status
paragraphs: Apttus does not have current, independent pricing because it is no longer sold as a standalone brand; the apttus.com domain now redirects directly to conga.com.
Prospective buyers researching Apttus's former CPQ and CLM products should evaluate Conga's current CPQ and CLM offerings directly, since that is where the underlying technology and support now live.
Historical Apttus pricing followed typical enterprise CPQ/CLM norms (custom, quote-based pricing tied to user counts and modules), but these figures are no longer relevant given the rebrand.

features.title: Contract lifecycle management (historical)
features.description: Apttus provided end-to-end CLM tools for drafting, negotiating, and managing contracts.

features.title: Configure-price-quote (historical)
features.description: Apttus CPQ helped sales teams configure complex products, apply pricing rules, and generate quotes quickly.

features.title: Native Salesforce integration (historical)
features.description: Many Apttus products were built natively on the Salesforce platform for tight CRM integration.

features.title: Revenue management (historical)
features.description: Apttus offered tools to manage revenue recognition and billing processes tied to contracts and quotes.

features.title: Merger with Conga
features.description: Apttus acquired and merged with Conga in 2020, adopting the Conga brand for the combined company.

features.title: Continued product lines under Conga
features.description: Former Apttus CPQ, CLM, and revenue products continue to be sold and developed today under the Conga brand.

pros: Historically a leading, well-funded CPQ and CLM vendor with deep Salesforce integration
Underlying technology and customer base continue today under the well-established Conga brand
Merger consolidated CPQ, CLM, document generation, and eSignature into a single vendor relationship for customers
cons: No longer exists as an independent brand; customers must engage with Conga directly
The apttus.com domain simply redirects, offering no live product information under the Apttus name
Historical Apttus-specific pricing and packaging are outdated and not representative of current Conga offerings
Anyone searching for Apttus support or pricing today must know to look for Conga instead

faqs.question: Does Apttus still exist as a company
faqs.answer: No. Apttus merged with Conga in 2020 and the combined company operates under the Conga name; apttus.com now redirects to conga.com.

faqs.question: What did Apttus do
faqs.answer: Apttus provided contract lifecycle management (CLM) and configure-price-quote (CPQ) software, built largely natively on the Salesforce platform.

faqs.question: Who founded Apttus
faqs.answer: Apttus was founded in 2006 by Kirk Krappe, Neehar Giri, and Kent Perkocha in San Mateo, California.

faqs.question: Why did Apttus become Conga
faqs.answer: Apttus acquired Conga in a 2020 deal valued at about 715 million US dollars and the combined company chose to operate under the Conga brand rather than the Apttus name.

faqs.question: Where can I find Apttus pricing today
faqs.answer: There is no current Apttus pricing; buyers should look at Conga's current CPQ and CLM pricing, since that is where the former Apttus products now live.

faqs.question: Is Conga the same company as Apttus
faqs.answer: Yes, in practice. Conga is the surviving brand of the 2020 Apttus-Conga merger, led by former Apttus CEO Frank Holland.

faqs.question: What products did Apttus offer
faqs.answer: Apttus offered CPQ, CLM, revenue management, and related quote-to-cash tools, all now continued under the Conga product line.

use_cases.title: Historical reference for quote-to-cash market research
use_cases.description: Analysts researching the CPQ and CLM software market reference Apttus as a foundational vendor prior to its merger into Conga.

use_cases.title: Understanding current Conga product origins
use_cases.description: Buyers evaluating Conga's CPQ and CLM tools benefit from knowing these product lines trace back to Apttus.

use_cases.title: Brand and domain redirect verification
use_cases.description: Teams doing vendor due diligence may need to confirm that apttus.com redirects to conga.com as part of verifying current company status.
